course_title: Theory and Society in the Pacific

learning_outcomes.description: Identify, evaluate and challenge how anthropology engages with Pacific societies, peoples, and cultures.
Demonstrate an awareness of Pasifika/Pacific perspectives that challenge historical biases and can be applied to engage ethically and respectfully with Pasifika peoples.
Demonstrate competence with contemporary anthropological concepts and scholarship in relation to key issues in the Pacific/Oceania region.

assessments.due_date:
19/08/2026 4:50 pm

The exam relies on students participating in a museum visit and then returning to the classroom to complete the written assessment. The written assessment is designed for completion within 60 minutes. Any additional class time will be available for students to complete.
assessments.task_description:
Students will visit the museum during class time (visit approx 60 minutes) and then return to the classroom to write an object-based critical research proposal (500 words max, approx 60 minutes).

The proposal will --

a. Describe the object you chose while undertaking the museum visit.

b. Using one area of debate or key concept from lectures and readings in week 1, 2 & 4 (e.g., mobility, personhood, commodity, value), explain a research question that might drive a research project about the museum object and its related peoples.

Students will write their responses by hand or by electronic submission (Inspera). Students will have access to 1 page (double sided) hard copy and/or offline notes, plus downloaded/printed copies of relevant readings from week 1, 2 & 4.

If students are absent due to illness, or other legitimate reason, an alternate time for writing will be scheduled.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The handwritten or online answer will be submitted by the end of the class time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
You will write two (2) reflections on the content of guest lecture modules (20% each) - 500 words max each, in class (60 mins)

  1. reflection week 5&6 Guest Dr Inez Fainga'a-Manu Sione - Pacific Communities and Cultures - completed in week 6
  2. reflection week 8&9 Guest Prof Kristen Lyons - Development and Climate Change - completed in week 9


Each reflection should be written in the first person (e.g. "While reading the work of Pacific scholar, Hau'ofa, I found the way he addressed the topic of..."), and include both clear knowledge of material at hand and personal learning process ("Reflecting on the questions of 'development', made me question... ).

For each topic/reading reflection, students will be assessed on how they -

  1. demonstrate engagement with readings and lecture content for the relevant weeks (summary/ content knowledge) 10/20
  2. demonstrate reflection on values, thoughts, feelings, and ideas on the topic at hand. For example, are your values or ideas shifting? Being challenged? What questions arise in ethical or political terms? How are you making sense of this learning process? 10/20


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must submit your assignment at the end of the class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
A final essay will rely on your review and critical discussion of Ethnographic films, informed by ethnographic film theory and practice, covered in weeks 10-12, by course readings throughout the course, and any other academic scholarship relevant to the course learning outcomes.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Worlds of theory and society. The Pacific : ideas, people, things and place

Introduction to the course, expectations and discussion of reading material for the week. Students are expected to read the required reading material before the class, and to bring notes and questions associated with those materials to the seminar.

Required and recommended readings for each week are available via the course list on the library website (search on "ANTH2020"). Please consult --

  1. The library course readings page for weekly required reading
  2. Blackboard for the document "Activities and reading summary" available on blackboard for detail of required and recommended readings
  3. Blackboard, weekly activities for questions that will guide what to focus on in relation to required and recommended readings


Required reading is Epeli Hau'ofa plus at least one other required reading, as listed.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
The current-contemporary Pacific: mobility, exchange, value

Pacific scholars and anthropologists challenge ideas of place, belonging, identity and mobility - What do these concepts mean in the history of scholarship the Pacific and how do they characterise the Pacific as a particular place about which to think about society, culture and reality?

We also examine and consider the history of concepts such as 'the gift', value, and commodity, and engage with material-focussed research questions. This class will contain detailed guidance to prepare students for the in-class assessment in Week 4

Required reading is Thomas (1991) plus at least one other of the listed articles.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: The current-contemporary Pacific: mobility, exchange, value Pacific scholars and anthropologists challenge ideas of place, belonging, identity and mobility - What do these concepts mean in the history of scholarship the Pacific and how do they characterise the Pacific as a particular place about which to think about society, culture and reality? We also examine and consider the history of concepts such as 'the gift', value, and commodity, and engage with material-focussed research questions. This class will contain detailed guidance to prepare students for the in-class assessment in Week 4 Required reading is Thomas (1991) plus at least one other of the listed articles.

learning_activities.topic:
Geopolitics, economics and race

Notions and contestations of race have always been present in anthropological engagements with Pacific peoples, and vice versa. How does race inform colonialism, geopolitics and contemporary economic relations in the Pacific?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Geopolitics, economics and race Notions and contestations of race have always been present in anthropological engagements with Pacific peoples, and vice versa. How does race inform colonialism, geopolitics and contemporary economic relations in the Pacific?

learning_activities.topic:
Ethnographic film 1: History, theory, and practice

This seminar will consider the genre of ethnographic film through the history of filmmaking and anthropology, the visual 'eye' of the lens for ethnography, particularly for Pacific peoples, and some of the ways that ethnographic film practice has changed more recently. There will be a short lecture followed by discussion.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Ethnographic film 1: History, theory, and practice This seminar will consider the genre of ethnographic film through the history of filmmaking and anthropology, the visual 'eye' of the lens for ethnography, particularly for Pacific peoples, and some of the ways that ethnographic film practice has changed more recently. There will be a short lecture followed by discussion.

learning_activities.topic:
Ethnographic film 2. Film: An Extraordinary Wedding & discussion

This class will involve watching the film "An Extraordinary Wedding" and discussing its content, ethics and aesthetics in the context of the history of ethnographic film.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

This class will involve watching the film "On Behalf of the living" and discussing its content, politics and relational message in the context of the history of ethnographic film.
